coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated on spinel nickel cobaltite nanocomposites for boosting supercapacitor electrode material performance
coredata.dc:creator: Kharangarh, Poonam R.
Ravindra, Nuggehalli M.
Rawal, Rachna
Singh, Amrita
Gupta, Vinay
coredata.dc:description: Composites of transition metal oxides, with carbon, have been considered to be appropriate materials for enhancing their electrochemical properties in supercapacitor applications. In this study, we prepare Nickel-Cobaltite/ Graphene Quantum Dots (NiCo2O4/GQDs) composite structures that exhibit improved electrical conductivity and function as electrode materials with higher energy density in comparison to GQDs and NiCo2O4. The electrochemical performance of NiCo2O4/GQDs is confirmed through galvanostatic charge–discharge method for three electrode systems with an electrolyte of 0.1 M potassium hydroxide. The observed specific capacitance for the fabricated composite has been found to be 481.4 Fg−1 at 0.35Ag−1. It is higher than that of Graphene Quantum Dots (Csp~45.6 Fg−1). This is due to the enhancement in the electrical conductivity and diffusion of ions which become faster between electrodes and electrolyte. These findings demonstrate the unique characteristics of the fabricated composites as superior electrode materials for applications in supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Hydrothermal method
Nickel-cobaltite/graphene quantum dots
Supercapacitor electrode
Potassium hydroxide

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of (a) GQDs; (b) NiCo2O4 nanoparticles; (c) NiCo2O4/GQDs composite; and (d) Interactions between NiCo2O4 and GQDs.

(a) X-Ray Diffraction patterns; (b) FT-IR; (c) Thermo Gravimetric Analysis (TGA) of GO, GQDs, NiCo2O3, NiCo2O3/GQDs composite; and (d) N2 adsorption & desorption isotherms and pore size distribution measurements (inset) of GQDs, NiCo2O3 and NiCo2O3/GQDs.
UV–Visible spectra of GO, GQDs, NiCo2O3 and NiCo2O3/GQDs.
Cyclic Voltammetry of (a) GQDs; (b) NiCo2O4; (c) NiCo2O4/GQDs at different scan rates; and (d) Nyquist Plot of NiCo2O4/GQDs where the inset of the figure shows the Nyquist Plot of GQDs, and NiCo2O4.

(a) Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves of NiCo2O4/GQDs composite at different current densities (b) at current density of 0.35 A/g of GQDs, NiCo2O4, NiCo2O4/GQDs; and (c) Cycling performance of fabricated NiCo2O4/GQDs composites.
Schematic diagram for the fabrication of NiCo2O4/GQDs composite.
Schematic Diagram for the redox behaviour during electrochemical reaction of NiCo2O4/GQDs composite.
Internal resistance (Ri), and Charge Transfer Resistance (Rct) and specific capacitance of GQDs, and NiCo2O4 and NiCo2O4/GQDs composites.
Comparison of specific capacitance values of different nanomaterials.

row.entry: flexible power source in the electronics
To develop high-voltage aqueous supercapacitors using in situ formed heterostructures

environmentally benign HNT based layered materials for the fabrication of supercapacitors
For flexible solar cell to build an integrated, self-sustaining power pack, for industrial manufacturing.

flexible solid-state power sources for future wearable electronics
to develop flexible solid-state textile energy storage devices
low-cost and green solution for textile based energy-storage devices.
Using “template-assisted method” with syntheziation of mixed transition metals and carbon materials for supercapacitors

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated on spinel nickel cobaltite nanocomposites for boosting supercapacitor electrode material performance

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Writing - original draft, Writing - review and editing, Writing - original draft

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Astrophysics, University of Delhi, Delhi 110007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Astrophysics, University of Delhi

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, New Jersey Institute of Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Physics and Astrophysics, University of Delhi, Delhi 110007, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Astrophysics, University of Delhi

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Composites of transition metal oxides, with carbon, have been considered to be appropriate materials for enhancing their electrochemical properties in supercapacitor applications. In this study, we prepare Nickel-Cobaltite/ Graphene Quantum Dots (NiCo2O4/GQDs) composite structures that exhibit improved electrical conductivity and function as electrode materials with higher energy density in comparison to GQDs and NiCo2O4. The electrochemical performance of NiCo2O4/GQDs is confirmed through galvanostatic charge–discharge method for three electrode systems with an electrolyte of 0.1 M potassium hydroxide. The observed specific capacitance for the fabricated composite has been found to be 481.4 Fg−1 at 0.35Ag−1. It is higher than that of Graphene Quantum Dots (Csp~45.6 Fg−1). This is due to the enhancement in the electrical conductivity and diffusion of ions which become faster between electrodes and electrolyte. These findings demonstrate the unique characteristics of the fabricated composites as superior electrode materials for applications in supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, energy storage devices have attracted significant attention because of the enormous growth in renewable energy sources such as solar and wind that has been accompanied by dispatchable generation. With the increase in global population, the demands on sources of energy has become a critical issue. This situation needs to be addressed without any delay. From this point of view, the exploration of some promising materials for brand spanking new energy storage devices for the long run is required. The well-known energy storage systems include “supercapacitors” (SCs), “Batteries”, and “Hybrid materials”. Several voltage storage devices, associated with “lead–acid batteries”, “nickel–metal binary compound batteries”, “Li ion batteries (LIBs)”, and SCs have been reported thus far in the literature [1]. Among them, the high energy density LIBs and the outstanding power density SCs are thought of to be emerging material structures, attributable to their low-price, and being environmental-friendly. These superior energy storage devices meet the requirements of the increasing demand in the industry. SCs will yield abundant high power density, long cycle life and quick charge–discharge processes compared to LIB with low energy density. In contrast, the LIBs will store significant energy density as compared to SCs. However, there has been a great demand for excellent and reliable energy storage devices. This is in view of the various advantages of “electronic devices” and “hybrid electrical vehicles”, which require high energy density and power density. In general, SCs are classified into three categories - “electrical double layer capacitors (EDLCs)”, pseudocapacitors and hybrid SCs [2]. Since EDLCs store charges using particle surface assimilation at the interface of carbon electrodes electrostatically, they demonstrate high performance of fast charge storage; however, they are restricted by low specific capacitance [3,4]. The well-known high surface area related to carbon electrodes include the following forms of carbon: “carbon fullerene (CNT)”, “activated carbon (AC)”, “porous carbon”, “carbon aerogel”, and “graphene”. On the other hand, pseudocapacitors mostly store charges from quick reversible oxidation and reduction reactions by different sorts of materials - i.e. transition metal oxides or hydroxides and conducting polymers. Conductive polymers exhibit enhanced electrical performance; however, their measured specific capacitances are found to be low in comparison to that of most metal oxides and their reduced electrochemical stability needs to be enhanced [5]. The reason for poor cycling stability is because of the degradation in the structural formation from the charge or discharge method [6]. Therefore, metal oxides are thought of to be the foremost favorable materials for superior pseudocapacitors [7–9] . These types of pseudocapacitors show high specific capacitance but have poor cycle stability [7]. Some common examples of transition metal oxides include “RuO2 [10,11], MnO2 [12–14], Ni(OH)2 [15] and conducting polymers such as polyaniline (PANI) [16], polypyrrole (PPy) [17], and ploy(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)(PEDOT)” [18], as well as graphene quantum dots [19]. Various properties of RuO2, including its high capacitance, quick reversible charge or discharge method and low price have led to its prescription for industrial applications [20]. Nowadays, in order to boost a high power density and elevated capacitance, a replacement style of SCs, referred to as Hybrid SCs, are being developed by combining the merits of EDLCs and pseudocapacitors [20].

A fast reversible redox reaction process, with multiple oxidization states of transition metal oxides such as NiO, NiCo2O4, and Co3O4, has been reported for the next-generation super capacitors [15,21–25]. These metal oxides are thought of as promising materials for ECs. This is due to the fact that the band gap of p-type semiconductors associated with NiO, NiCo2O4, CdS,Co3O4, and CeO2 are 3.6 eV [26], 2.1 eV [27], 3.6 eV [28], 2.2 eV [29] and 3.2 eV [30], respectively. Thus far, these transition metal oxides have lower specific capacitance compared to that of RuO2. Much work needs to be done to improve the electrical behavior of RuO 2 . For better performance of the electrochemical behavior of these metal oxides based electrodes, their morphology, and electro-physical properties must be taken into account. As is known in the literature, the electro-physical phenomenon plays a crucial role for the quick transfer of ions and reduces the internal resistance of the operating electrodes. The introduction of impurities or defects is feasible because of the doping of metals with different semiconductors which reinforces the electro-physical behavior of the conductor material. Suitable structures will be accountable for high specific area and will provide a simple solution. For these reasons, it becomes crucial to tailor the morphology in terms of microstructure, crystalline nature, and electro-physical behavior of these mostly oxides based materials in EC connected applications.

However, for further development in renewable energy systems, the electrode materials based on NiCo2O4 are significantly limited in their functionality due to their reduced electronic conductivity. The amalgamation of carbon materials is being proposed as an alternate approach to overcome the limitations of NiCo2O4 [31–37] .

The most investigated materials for asymmetric supercapacitors include the following: hierarchical NiCo2O4 nanostructures on flexible carbon cloth (CC){NiCo2O4/CC} [38], graphene quantum dot (GQD)/MnO2 heterostructural electrodes [39], CoCl2 doped graphene quantum dots (COCl2-GQDs) [40], Fe (II)S-GQDs [41], “hierarchical N-doped porous carbon onto a carbonized metal–organic framework (cMOF-5) [42], nanofiber composed of poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) [43], ”core (active material)-shell (conductive material)-type Mn3O4-graphene QDs [44], GQD–3DG composite [45], halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [46], Fe2O3 QDs/FGS composites[47], “graphene oxide quantum dot-derived nitrogen-enriched hybrid graphene nanosheets” [48], activated paper carbon/reduced graphene oxide/ Sulfur (APC/graphene/S)[49], activated banana peel (ABP) (negative electrode) and NiCo2O4/ABP (positive electrode) asymmetric[50], Co-Al LDH/ACT//ACT/ graphene asymmetric[51], flexible ACT/NiS2-graphene hybrid Anode[52], flexible asymmetric CoO@NiO/ACT//ACT/graphene[53], flexible NiCo2O4@NiCo2O4/ACT[54], flexible MnO2/ZTO/CMF hybrid [55], ACT//MnO2/ACT [56], NiCo2O4@RGO [57–62], and NiCo2O4@graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composite [63].

Although a significant amount of work on doping of metals and oxides in GQDs has been published in the literature, the influence of NiCo2O4 on GQDs to produce composite materials as SC electrodes, with highly specific characteristics, has not been reported.

In this paper, we report the development of Nickel-Cobaltite/Graphene Quantum Dots (NiCo2O 4 /GQDs) composite structures as supercapacitor (SC) electrodes. An investigation into their applications in energy storage devices is presented. The fabricated high quality NiCo2O4/GQD, by utilizing hydrothermal method, is essential to increase the capacity and long life cycle of the material. The required properties include high power density, fast charging and discharging and long life cycle. The fabricated composite material structures show higher specific capacitance than other transition metal based oxide materials. In addition, the possible oxidation states or loss of electrons has been improved by adding both nickel and cobalt ions.

“Graphite powder (Graphite India)”, “NaNO3 (purity ~99%,)”, “KMnO4, (purity~99%)”, “KOH (purity~99.99%)”, “30% H2O2” and “HCl (purity~98%)”, “Ni (NO3)2•6H2O (purity>99%)”, “Co (NO3)2•6H2O (purity>98%)”, and “Urea (purity>98%)” were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Double-distilled (DD) water with ρ = 18.2 MΩ·cm was used in all the experiments as well as in the purification.

The entire fabrication method is shown in schematic diagram ( Scheme 1) which incorporates three steps. The first step includes the preparation of graphene oxide (GO) that has been synthesized by modified Hummers and Offemann method [64]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are prepared by the hydrothermal method, as briefly discussed in our earlier reported work [65–67]. For the second step, the synthesis of nano-particles of NiCo2O4 was done by using sol-gel technique. In order to synthesize NiCo2O4, two different powders viz. Ni(NO3)2•6H2O (0.2 mol), Co(NO3)2•6H2O (0.4 mol) were dissolved in 30 mL solution of urea (1 mol in 30 mL DW). The quantitative ratio of 1:1 for metal ion and citric acid was maintained to arrange the hybrid spheres. The ensuing mixture was then stirred for 30 mins at 90 °C to make the gel. This prepared gel was again stirred for an additional three hours for the preparation of proper viscous gel. The product was calcined for 10hrs at 850°C to remove organic moieties. The ensuing sample NiCo2O4 was cooled to room temperature (RT) in air. For the third step, our aim was to synthesize NiCo2O4/GQD composites for energy storage applications. Next, the composite material, NiCo2O4 (50 mg) and GQDs (50 mg) were dissolved in 40 mL of solution and transferred in Teflon-autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and heated at 180°C for 8hrs. The as-prepared sample was washed with deionized water (DI), acetone, hot water and ethanol, numerous times and dehydrated at 60°C for twelve hrs.

The samples were measured by various characterization techniques including “High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy, HRTEM, Powdered X-Ray Diffraction (PXRD), Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area measurements and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH)” pore size distribution [41]. Electrochemical measurements were performed by using an “electrochemical work station (CHI760D)” with “three-electrode system” in a “0.1 M KOH aqueous electrolyte at room temperature (RT)”. A similar method has been followed for the preparation of working electrodes [41].

Fig. 1(a–c) show TEM images of GQDs, NiCo2O4 nanoparticles and NiCo2O4/GQDs composites. HRTEM images of NiCo2O4/GQDs composites are displayed in Fig. 1(d) with inset of Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED). As shown in Fig. 1(a), the spherical shaped GQDs, with well uniform size distribution, in the range of 20–50 nm, are found after hydrothermal treatment by cutting the micrometer graphene sheets into small fragments. Fig. 1(b) reveals that the NiCo2O4 nanoparticles, synthesized by sol gel method, are in the size range of 50–100 nm.

Fig. 1(c) shows that the well-distributed particles of NiCo2O4/GQDs, within a size range of 5–10 nm, are found to be much smaller in comparison to that of GQDs and NiCo2O4 nanoparticles. The morphology and size of NiCo2O4/GQDs is found to be more significant than that of NiCo2O4 or GQDs. A large number of clusters of particles are found in GQDs, NiCo2O4 displayed in Fig. 1(a) and (b), suggesting that the introduction of GQDs (0.24 nm) makes NiCo2O4 further less crystalline in nature with an increment in the quantity of binding and conducting agents. It favors the development of agglomerates in the NiCo2O4/GQDs. This is the result of electrostatic, dipole-dipole interaction and hydrogen bonding related agents that are present due to functional groups on the surface during hydrothermal treatment supporting the formation of quantum dots of graphene and interacting with Ni(II) and Co(III) cations and urea. This mechanism changes the growth of the crystal and thus alters the morphologies of the final prepared material. Strong chemical interactions were observed between NiCo2O4 and the residual oxygen-containing functional groups on the GQDs, or van der Waals interactions between NiCo2O4 and the GQDs in the NiCo2O4/GQDs composite [shown in Fig. 1(d)]. The obtained results confirm that the addition of GQDs in NiCo2O4 samples lead to improvement in the electrochemical performance of the fabricated NiCo2O4/GQDs composite.

XRD measurements were employed for determining the crystalline nature of GO, GQDs, NiCo2O4, and NiCo2O4/GQDs composite, and comparing them. Fig. 2(a) shows the XRD patterns of the synthesized GO (modified Hummers method), GQDs (hydrothermal reduction of GO), NiCo2O4 (Sol Gel method) and NiCo2O4/GQDs (hydrothermal treatment) composites. The observed Bragg peak of GO was found near 2θ = 10.9° confirming the complete oxidation with the existence of (001) plane. The presence of wide Bragg peak in GO is due to oxygen related functional groups on graphene sheets. Thus, both the distance and disorder in the carbon atom structure (atomic scale) increases between graphene sheets. Subsequent to the hydrothermal process, the modified broad Bragg peak reveals the conversion of “GO to GQDs” due to change in size and edge effects. The wide peak in GQD [(002) plane], detected at 2θ = 26.7ᵒ, is related to prominent crystalline structure such as graphitic structure which might be possible due to discontinuity in the regular stacking [41]. The observed characteristic peak in GQDs is found to be similar to graphene (2θ = 26.3°) indicating that the oxygen related functional groups was removed and the graphene structure was restored after giving heat treatment or by thermal reduction. The presence of the diffraction peaks (111), (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440) in the pattern confirm the existence of NiCo2O4 which agrees with the standard pattern for NiCo2O4 (JCPDS card No. 73-1702) [68] and is assigned as the “inverse spinel structure” of NiCo2O4 [33]. Moreover, the appearance of diffraction peaks (200), (002), (111), (220), (311), (400), (411), (133), and (511) in the patterns confirm that the prepared composite was successfully fabricated. The composite with broad peak at 2θ = 26.7° has been significantly enhanced with GQDs indicating that the fragments of graphene were stacked in a disordered manner. However, a reduction in the intensity of peaks in NiCo2O4/GQDs composites implies that the lower crystallinity provides the satisfactory path for penetration and diffusion of ions. This confirms that the fabricated composite electrode demonstrates a high performance of capacitive behavior.

In order to confirm the presence of functional groups, FT-IR spectroscopy was utilized to detect the bands of GO, GQDs, NiCo2O4, and NiCo2O4/GQDs composite as shown in Fig. 2(b). FTIR spectra, shown in Fig. 2(b), reveal that the “vibrational peaks” of GO and GQDs are quiet similar, as discussed in our earlier reported work [41]. Some other peaks were apparent for NiCo2O4 and NiCo2O4/GQDs composite. The detected “vibrational bands” of NiCo2O4, were observed at 445, 554, 636, 852, 1073, 1260, 1461, 1771 cm−1, whereas the identified “vibrational peaks” of NiCo2O4/GQDs composite were found at 559, 641, 704, 736, 852, 911, 1066, 1351, 1442, 1558, 1765 cm−1. The vibrational peak at 1442 cm−1 (shifted from 1461 cm−1) in NiCo2O4/GQD composites was observed to be of low intensity. It might be due to the presence of oxygen related functional groups. Also, a shift in the vibrational frequencies of the carbonate group from 1461 cm−1 in the precursor samples indicates the possibility for carbonate ions to act as anionic spices for the as prepared NiCo2O4/GQDs composites. Two characteristic strong bands were perceived in NiCo2O4 and NiCo2O4/GQDs composite at “lower frequencies (~553 and 641 cm–1)”. They can be assigned to the “stretching vibrations of the Co-O and Ni-O bonds in nickel cobalt oxide” [69]. The observed alcoholic groups were significantly reduced or vanished in NiCo2O4/GQDs composite at higher frequency. The obtained results agree that the bonding of nickel and cobalt atoms is similar to former informed outcomes [69] that relate to the interaction of Ni and Co related active ions on the surface of graphene sheet.

Fig. 2(c) shows the results of thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) for synthesized GO, GQDs, NiCo2O4, and NiCo2O4/GQDs. These experiments were performed to verify the thermal stability of the samples and were carried out at temperatures from room to 800 °C. The extreme weight loss from the TGA graph was identified for GO (~20% and 25%) to be near 100 °C, GQDs (32% and 13%) in the range of 150–300 °C, NiCo2O4 (7%, 6%, and 50%) to be in between 400–800 °C and NiCo2O4/GQDs (8%, and 23%) from 150° to 200°C and 600–650 °C. These results can be attributed to the following factors: (a). trapped water molecules that get evaporated (Temperature< 150 °C) and (b). thermal decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups in the samples. In NiCo2O4/GQDs composites, the obtained weight loss between 300 °C and 600 °C corresponds to burning of graphene flakes to fragments, i.e. from GQDs in the NiCo2O4/GQDs composite due to exothermal reaction. Hence, the observed weight loss in the final product (NiCo2O4/GQDs) is found to be lower in comparison to the precursor material (GO). This reveals that the composite (NiCo2O4/GQDs) has a good thermal stability due to the presence of Ni and Co related moieties.

In order to investigate the mesoporous characteristics of GO, GQDs, NiCo2O4, and NiCo2O4/GQDs, “N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore size distribution measurements” (inset) are shown in Fig. 2(d). From Fig. 2(d), the existence of “hysteresis loop” of type H3 in the relative pressure (P/Po) range of 0.50–0.99 reveals its porous structure. Moreover, from BET analysis, the specific surface area of GQDs, NiCo2O4, and NiCo2O4/GQDs, respectively, were 21, 40.6 and 168.8 m2g−1. These results suggest that, amongst the samples considered in this study, the composite related to NiCo2O4/GQDs has the maximum specific surface area. Materials that exhibit this property of high surface area are very useful as high performance electrodes in electrochemical energy storage devices. The values of “pore size distribution” of NiCo2O4/GQDs are 2.54, 3.75, and 6.2 nm; thus, they exhibit nanopores (with a narrow pore size distribution). 18.8 nm corresponds to mesopores (wide pore size distribution) which suggests a hierarchical mesoporous structure. Moreover, the diffusion of ions between NiCo2O4/GQDs composite and KOH aqueous electrolyte becomes much faster due to the increase in active sites and various defects/impurities that are attached to the edge as well as the nano size effect. These factors provide sufficient trails to support charge transport by carrying charges into interspace, thereby making the same composite to be an excellent electrode material for supercapacitor applications.

“UV–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra” of GO, GQDs, NiCo2O4, and NiCo2O4/GQDs composite “in aqueous media” were recorded and are shown in Fig. 3. In these spectra, two peaks were observed; they are located at 212 nm (“π-π* transitions of aromatic C═C bonds”) and 251 nm (“n-π* transitions of C═O bond”) in GO as reported earlier [41]. A shift in the absorption peaks for undoped GQDs was detected at 260 (red shift) and 320 nm (n band). The presence of the n band relates to the “oxygen related functional groups” in the middle of π and π* bands. NiCo2O4, prepared by sol gel method, shows that the peaks shift again at 237 nm and 301 nm. Subsequent to doping, the synthesized material NiCo2O4 in GQDs is called as NiCo2O4/GQDs; a blue shift can be seen at 209 nm and 243 nm. Furthermore, the absorption peak at 209 nm corresponds to “π-π* transitions” and 243 nm exhibits “n-π* transitions” in the NiCo2O4/GQDs composite due to the transition to the “excited state”. This corresponds to trapping by the “surface state of GQDs” that are amalgamated with impurities related to cobalt and nickel, ensuing in strong emission. As a result, the observed higher absorption of NiCo2O4/GQDs composite, in comparison to GQDs or GO, improves the absorption in visible light pathways.

Cyclic voltammograms (CV) measurements were performed to evaluate the electrochemical performance of GO, GQDs, NiCo2O4, and NiCo2O4/GQDs composite by using three electrode system including a “glassy electrode (GE)” as a “working electrode”, “platinum wire as a counter electrode” and “silver (Ag/AgCl) as a reference electrode”. Before electrochemical deposition, GE was cleaned using acetone and isopropyl alcohol for some time. The preparation of slurry was done by mixing the prepared composite NiCo2O4/GQDs as an active material (wt~75%), “acetylene black carbon” (wt~15%) and “polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)” (wt~10%) with a few droplets of acetone in a small beaker (5 mL). As a consequence, the prepared powder transformed into a glassy electrode whose surface area was ~0.07 cm 2
. The working electrode was dried in an open atmosphere. Cyclic voltammograms (CV) were measured at scan rates of 20–100 mVs−1 in a range of potential −1.0–1.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed to study the “ohmic resistance of the electrode system” which could provide information relating to the “redox behavior and equivalent series resistance”. The specific capacitance (Fg−1), according to CV and GCD, and internal resistance (Ri) were calculated by using the same general formula as in ref [70].

Fig. 4(a–c) show the CV of GQDs, NiCo2O4, and NiCo2O4/GQDs recorded at various scan rates from 20 to 100 mV s−1 with an equal interval of 20 mVs−1. The observed redox peaks in each CV plot reveals that the calculated capacitance is mostly based on the redox behavior. The observed “strong anodic peak at 0.16 V” and the corresponding “cathodic peak at 0.27 V” in GQDs [Fig. 4(a)] were assigned to the change in the redox behavior. Interestingly, Fig. 4(b) shows a CV curve with strong anodic peak in NiCo2O4 at 0.38 V, and the corresponding “cathodic peak was observed at 0.20 V”; which is found to be similar to the CV nature of nickel cobaltite NiCo2O4 at a specific potential (Ep = 400 mV). We assume that this couple of redox peaks are allocated to the redox reactions of Co(II)/Co(III) at 0.417 V [71] and Ni (II)/Ni (III) at 0.217 V [72]. From these CV measurements, the peak due to Ni(II)/Ni(III) was not observed because of the lower annealing temperatures for the prepared samples during fabrication. The reduction in its peak indicates that the “Ni(II) and Ni(III) states are less stable in comparison to the other metal ions related to Co (II)/Co(III). Additionally, Co–C bonds are appreciably more stable than Ni‒C bonds”. The observed peaks confirm that the fabricated NiCo2O4 composite is mainly due to Co(II)/Co(III) transitions [72]. This redox behavior corresponds to the formation for the following reaction of CoO2/CoOOH & NiO2/CoOOH on the surface of cobaltite under potential cycling conditions shown in Scheme 2.

Fig. 4(c) shows the CV curves of NiCo2O4/GQDs composite; a quasi-rectangular pattern, with high specific capacitance, at different scan rates (10–100 mVs−1) [71] is observed. The observed high capacitance determines the low resistance with significant charge movement in capacitive behavior. It might be attributed to the presence of both Co and Ni ions due to their possible oxidation states in various metal oxides.

NiCo2O4/GQDs composite from CV plot demonstrates a larger integrated CV area with broad redox peaks in comparison to NiCo2O4 and GQDs. The obtained results show that the presence of more efficient redox active sites enhances the electrochemical properties supporting the rapid diffusion of electrons and ions at the electrolyte/electrode interface. As a result, the increased conductivity produces the conductive path for electrons/ions due to a strong C─O─Ni/Co interaction; this improves the electrochemical performance. Also, the current density and the long-term maintenance of reactivity increases due to this strong C─O─Ni/Co interaction at the interface between NiCo2O4 and GQDs.

In addition, the calculated SCs of the NiCo2O4/GQDs are found to be higher in comparison to GQDs and NiCo2O4. These calculations have been performed by using equations as reported in reference [70]. The results of these calculations are summarized in Table 1. However, the higher SCs of NiCo2O4/GQDs can be attributed to the fragments of graphene or GQDs and their edge effect that help to increase the ion diffusion and thereby, be responsible for high electrochemical reaction between the electron transport of active materials and the efficient ions for the EDLC.

Fig. 4(d) represents the Nyquist plot of NiCo2O4/GQDs; the inset figure shows the Nyquist plot of GQDs and NiCo2O4. Since the internal resistance (Ri), relates to the cumulative resistances (ionic resistance of electrolyte + intrinsic & contact resistance at the interface of fabricated material), it can be found from the intercept; “charge transfer resistance (Rct)” corresponds to the semicircle of the graphs. Table 1 shows the calculated values of Ri and Rct of the GQDs, NiCo2O4, and NiCo2O4/GQDs composite electrodes. Apparently, the NiCo2O4/GQDs electrode demonstrates lesser value of Ri and Rct in comparison to NiCo2O4 (Ω) and GQDs (kΩ) electrodes due to the increase in conductivity of electrons in NiCo2O4/GQDs composite. These results are consistent with the band theory in semiconductor physics. The introduction of impurity in situ mixed together with these oxides leads to improvement in the conductivity of moving electrons in the composite material. The NiCo2O4/GQDs composite electrode has lower resistance due to its active mesoporous structure; this helps to increase the ion diffusion of electrolyte in the fabricated composite electrode as compared to GQDs and NiCo2O4.

Galvanostatic discharge curves of the GQDs/NiCo2O4 composite electrode, at various current densities, are shown in Fig. 5. The calculated specific capacitances, based on Fig. 5(a) of the GQDs/NiCo2O4 composite electrode, are 481.4, 331.5, 238.3, 221Fg−1 at current densities of 0.35, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0 mAg−1, respectively. The GQDs/NiCo2O4 composite electrode reveals a recovered rate performance compared with some reported NiCo2O4-based electrode materials. These results are presented in a comparison Table ( Table 2). In order to determine the specific capacitance due to NiCo2O4/GQDs composite electrode and recognize the corresponding rate capability, charging and discharging measurements were performed. Fig. 5(b) shows the charge and discharge curves of the fabricated composite electrode i.e. NiCo2O4/GQDs, at a current density 0.35 Ag−1. Firstly, we observed that charge transfer increased due to increase in electron conductivity in the composite electrode. Secondly, the large surface area and more additional active sites in effective mesoporous structure significantly increase the use and pseudo capacitance of active materials.

Fig. 5(c) shows the long term cycle stability of the GQDs, NiCo2O4, and NiCo2O4/GQDs composite electrode at a particular current density of 0.35 Ag−1 for SC applications. The increased specific capacitance in NiCo2O4/GQDs composite electrode is due to the entire stimulation of the active materials in comparison to GQDs and NiCo2O4. After 300 cycles, there is a gradual decrease in capacitance with increase in number of cycles. The obtained cycle stability of the prepared electrode decreases by approximately 65.88% from its initial state. NiCo2O4/GQDs composite electrode shows excellent stability. This is a significant step in the design and fabrication of high-performance pseudo capacitors.

In the above study, NiCo2O4/GQD composites, synthesized by hydrothermal method, have been shown to be a promising electrode material in energy storage applications. Strong interaction between NiCo2O4 and GQDs demonstrated a “fast and reversible redox reaction” and exhibits a high “specific capacitance”. The low cost, easy fabrication by bottom up method and environmentally friendly NiCo2O4/GQD composite based electrode material, with high conductivity, will be a promising substitute material for the next generation electrochemical supercapacitors. These materials will also be beneficial for backup power in portable electronic devices.

Poonam R. Kharangarh*: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft preparation. Vinay Gupta*: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, and Supervision. Nuggehalli M Ravindra: Writing - review and editing, Writing - original draft preparation. Rachna Rawal: Writing - review & editing. Amrita Singh: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Department of Science & Technology, New Delhi, India, under Women Scientists Scheme-A (WOS-A), reference number (SR/WOS-A/PM-107/2017). The authors thank the University Science Instrumentation Centre (USIC), and the Department of Chemistry, University of Delhi, Delhi, India for providing the experimental facilities for materials characterization including PXRD, BET and TGA/DTA.
